---
title: "Hello, Babashka"
author:
  - name: Daniel Slutsky
    url: https://scicloj.github.io/contributors/daslu/
    image: https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5673102?v=4
    links:
      - {icon: github, href: 'https://github.com/daslu'}
type: post
date: '2026-05-01'
category: libs
tags: [babashka, babqua, quarto]
draft: true
filters:
  - bb
---

This is the first [Babashka](https://babashka.org)-authored post on Clojure Civitas — and at the same time, a small walkthrough of how to author your own. The page is rendered with [Babqua](https://github.com/scicloj/babqua), a [Quarto](https://quarto.org) extension that evaluates `{.clojure .bb}` code blocks with [Babashka](https://babashka.org) during the render and replaces each block with its evaluated output. [Kindly](https://scicloj.github.io/kindly-noted/kindly) metadata controls how each value renders, the same convention [Clay](https://scicloj.github.io/clay/) uses for [Clojure](https://clojure.org) documents.

Babqua is a sibling of [Janqua](https://github.com/scicloj/janqua), which does the same for [Jank](https://jank-lang.org). The two extensions deliberately share authoring conventions — fence syntax, Kindly kinds, frontmatter shape — so a post can move between languages without rewriting its scaffolding.

A huge thanks to [Michiel Borkent](https://github.com/borkdude) and the wider Babashka community for the runtime that makes this possible.

## Why Babashka, on Civitas?

[Babashka](https://babashka.org) is a [Clojure](https://clojure.org) interpreter aimed at scripting and shell tasks. It starts in milliseconds, runs as a single binary, and includes a wide set of built-in libraries — [JSON](https://github.com/dakrone/cheshire), [Hiccup](https://github.com/weavejester/hiccup), [HTTP](https://github.com/babashka/http-client), [filesystem](https://github.com/babashka/fs), [process](https://github.com/babashka/process), and more. The [pods ecosystem](https://github.com/babashka/babashka/blob/master/doc/projects.md#pods) extends it further (SQLite, AWS, HTML parsing, …) without changing the deployment story.

For a notebook-style post, the appeal is reproducibility: a `.qmd` file plus a `bb.edn` (when it needs extra deps) is enough to render anywhere `bb` is installed. No JVM warm-up, no project skeleton.

## A first taste

Code blocks without Kindly metadata render as code output. The simplest possible block:

```{.clojure .bb}
(+ 1 2 3)
```

Annotate a value with `^:kind/...` to tell Babqua how to render it:

```{.clojure .bb}
^:kind/hiccup
[:div {:style "color: coral; font-size: 24px;"}
  "Hello from Babashka!"]
```

## A small taste of the batteries

[Cheshire](https://github.com/dakrone/cheshire) is built in. Parsing a small inline JSON blob and walking the result is one call:

```{.clojure .bb}
(require '[cheshire.core :as json])

(def cities
  (json/parse-string
   "[{\"city\":\"Tokyo\",      \"millions\":37},
     {\"city\":\"Delhi\",      \"millions\":33},
     {\"city\":\"Shanghai\",   \"millions\":29},
     {\"city\":\"São Paulo\",  \"millions\":22},
     {\"city\":\"Mexico City\",\"millions\":22}]"
   true))

(map :city cities)
```

State carries across blocks the way it does in any [REPL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Read%E2%80%93eval%E2%80%93print_loop) session — `cities` defined in one block is visible in the next.

The same data can be passed to [Vega-Lite](https://vega.github.io/vega-lite/) by way of `^:kind/vega-lite`. Babqua serializes the value to JSON and Vega renders it client-side:

```{.clojure .bb}
^:kind/vega-lite
{:data {:values cities}
 :mark {:type "bar" :color "coral"}
 :encoding {:x {:field "city" :type "nominal" :sort "-y"}
            :y {:field "millions" :type "quantitative"
                :title "population (millions)"}}
 :width 360
 :height 220}
```

[`babashka.process`](https://github.com/babashka/process) is also built in, and shells out to system tools the way a Bash script would — useful for posts that drive `git`, `curl`, or any command-line program. A trivial example:

```{.clojure .bb}
(require '[babashka.process :as p])

(-> (p/shell {:out :string} "bb" "--version")
    :out
    clojure.string/trim)
```

## Authoring a Babashka post on Civitas

The path from "I want to write a Babashka post" to "PR open":

### 1. Prerequisites

Beyond the standard Civitas setup (Quarto, Java 21, [Clojure CLI](https://clojure.org/guides/install_clojure)), Babashka posts need only one extra tool:

- [Babashka](https://babashka.org/) (`bb`) — see the [install instructions](https://github.com/babashka/babashka#installation) for your system.

If your post needs extra Clojure libraries or [pods](https://github.com/babashka/babashka/blob/master/doc/projects.md#pods), drop a `bb.edn` next to the post (or at the project root) — Babashka picks it up automatically.

If you only contribute Clojure posts and skip Babashka, you can still build the site — Babashka pages will surface in-page error blocks where evaluated output should be, and the rest of the site renders normally.

### 2. Create the post

Drop a `.qmd` file at `src/<topic>/<post>.qmd` with a Babqua-aware frontmatter:

```yaml
---
title: "My Babashka Post"
author:
  - name: Your Name
type: post
date: '2026-05-01'
category: libs
tags: [babashka, ...]
draft: true
filters:
  - bb
---
```

Then write the body. `{.clojure .bb}` fenced blocks are evaluated; `.clojure` enables syntax highlighting, `.bb` triggers evaluation. Use Kindly metadata (`^:kind/hiccup`, `^:kind/vega-lite`, `^:kind/plotly`, `^:kind/mermaid`, `^:kind/table`, …) to control the rendering of each value. The [Babqua docs](https://scicloj.github.io/babqua/) list the full set of supported kinds and per-block options.

### 3. Iterate with fast single-file preview

```bash
quarto preview src/<topic>/<post>.qmd
```

This works because the repo is already set up for it: a `_extensions` symlink at the root pointing to `site/_extensions/` (where the Babqua extension lives), and a minimal `_quarto.yml` that lets Quarto find the filter without dragging in the full-website context. Symlinks recreate automatically on Linux/macOS; on Windows you may need `git config --global core.symlinks true` plus Developer Mode.

A live-reload server starts; saves trigger a re-render. Each render spawns a fresh `bb` process by default — fast enough for most posts. If your post does expensive setup (loads a pod, fetches data), you can opt into a persistent Babashka session that keeps state warm across saves:

```bash
# Start once per session, before `quarto preview`
bb site/_extensions/scicloj/bb/babqua-lifecycle.bb start

# Run preview as normal — the filter detects the live REPL and routes
# evaluations through it.
quarto preview src/<topic>/<post>.qmd

# When you're done, stop it.
bb site/_extensions/scicloj/bb/babqua-lifecycle.bb stop
```

While a persistent session is running, `def`s and `require`s carry across renders the way they would in any Clojure REPL. The session creates three lifecycle dotfiles at the repo root (`.babqua-pid`, `.babqua-nrepl-port`, `.babqua-bb.log`) — they're gitignored. See the [Workflow chapter of the Babqua docs](https://scicloj.github.io/babqua/workflow.html) for the full set of lifecycle controls.

### 4. (Optional) Full-site preview

If you want to see the post inside the actual site — theme, listings, navigation — you can render it in full-site context:

```bash
bb preview
```

This runs Clay and then `quarto preview site` on the whole website. It can be quite slow on some machines (the site has many pages); for routine iteration, step 3's single-file preview is much faster.

### 5. Open a PR

```bash
git add src/<topic>/<post>.qmd
git commit -m "blog: ..."
git push origin <branch>
gh pr create --base main
```

CI already has Babashka installed (Janqua needed it for `clj-nrepl-eval`, and Babqua reuses the same binary), so no extra workflow changes are needed. `draft: true` keeps the post out of the public posts listing while you iterate; drop the line when you're ready to publish.

## Where next?

This post was the first Babashka-authored page on Civitas. We'd love to see more. Babashka is well suited to small, scriptable demonstrations — fetching data, walking a directory, calling a pod, parsing a log — and posts that exercise one of those corners are exactly the kind of thing that makes the language and its ecosystem more discoverable.

If you hit something that doesn't work, please open an issue on [Babqua](https://github.com/scicloj/babqua/issues) or on [Civitas](https://github.com/ClojureCivitas/clojurecivitas.github.io/issues). Feedback shapes both.
